title: The Mouthful Neighborhood
summary: How the oral microbiome works — a small, busy neighborhood with hundreds of microbes that balance each other. Trauma-informed register: brushing is care, not a moral test; cavities are ecology, not blame.

questions.prompt: The oral microbiome is the community of microbes that live in…
choices: Your mouth — on teeth, gums, tongue, and cheeks
questions.explanation: Your mouth hosts hundreds of microbe species — bacteria, yeasts, even some viruses. Together they make up the oral microbiome.

questions.prompt: Streptococcus mutans is a common oral microbe that gets more attention because it…
choices: Loves sugar and can turn it into acid that softens tooth enamel
questions.explanation: Strep. mutans isn't 'bad' on its own — it just thrives on sugar. When sugar is around a lot, it makes acid, and that acid can wear on enamel over time.

questions.prompt: Brushing your teeth helps the oral microbiome by…
choices: Gently lowering the layer of plaque so no single microbe takes over
Removing every microbe in your mouth forever
Stopping all helpful microbes from growing back
Replacing your saliva with new saliva
questions.explanation: Brushing doesn't sterilize your mouth — it gently thins the plaque layer so the community stays balanced. Helpful microbes regrow quickly.

questions.prompt: Saliva helps keep the oral microbiome balanced because it…
choices: Washes microbes gently and carries minerals that strengthen enamel
Is the same as stomach acid
questions.explanation: Saliva is one of the body's quietest helpers — it rinses food bits, carries calcium and phosphate to enamel, and keeps the mouth chemistry friendly to balance.

questions.prompt: Drinking water after a sugary snack helps the oral neighborhood because it…
choices: Rinses leftover sugar so acid-making microbes have less to work with
Adds new bacteria to your mouth
questions.explanation: A quick sip of water after sweets is one of the simplest microbiome-friendly habits — less leftover sugar means less acid time for enamel.

questions.prompt: Most microbes in a healthy mouth are…
choices: Quiet members of a balanced community that help keep things steady
questions.explanation: Like the gut, the mouth is mostly home to quiet, balanced microbes. The 'cavity-causing' ones are part of the same neighborhood — they just respond strongly to sugar and time.
